coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of fluorescent CQDs/45S5 bioactive nanohybrids as bone targeted drug carriers

coredata.dc:creator: parsaeifar, Nafiseh
Salimi, Esmaeil
Molaei, Mohammad Jafar
coredata.dc:description: Observing the degradation rate of the bone fillers upon implantation enables us to predict the next stages of healing process. Strong luminescent property of the biocompatible carbon quantum dots accompanied by the high bioactivity of the 45S5 bioglass, bearing an antibiotic drug, can form reliable bone filler with detectable degradation rate via bioimaging. This study reported the preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots/45S5 (CQDs/45S5) nanohybrids via the chemical precipitation procedure for the first time. The pyrolysis of the citric acid was carried out to achieve the fluorescent N-doped CQDs with an average size of around 11 nm. The physicochemical and microstructural characteristics of the obtained materials were studied by XRD, FTIR and FESEM, respectively, Ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies were employed to investigate the features of the carbon dots as well as hybrid materials. The PL spectroscopy analysis indicated the in-vitro luminescent emission of the CQDs/45S5 nanohybrids compared to 45S5 bioglass. Around 22 μg of DOX could adhere to 1 mg of the nanohybrids after 12 h of contact, proposed that more quantity of particles were needed to adsorb higher amount of drug. The drug release study indicated a fast release pattern of DOX (90 %) during the first hours. The in-vitro bioactivity and biocompatibility results revealed the high capability of the BG/15 mL CQDs sample to deposit calcium phosphate with no toxic effects on the fibroblast cells. Consequently, the obtained nanohybrids could be promising trackable drug-vehicle candidates, as well as bone fillers, bone cements and implant coating materials.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of fluorescent CQDs/45S5 bioactive nanohybrids as bone targeted drug carriers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, P. O. Box:3619995161, Shahrood, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, P. O. Box:3619995161, Shahrood, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Observing the degradation rate of the bone fillers upon implantation enables us to predict the next stages of healing process. Strong luminescent property of the biocompatible carbon quantum dots accompanied by the high bioactivity of the 45S5 bioglass, bearing an antibiotic drug, can form reliable bone filler with detectable degradation rate via bioimaging. This study reported the preparation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots/45S5 (CQDs/45S5) nanohybrids via the chemical precipitation procedure for the first time. The pyrolysis of the citric acid was carried out to achieve the fluorescent N-doped CQDs with an average size of around 11 nm. The physicochemical and microstructural characteristics of the obtained materials were studied by XRD, FTIR and FESEM, respectively, Ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies were employed to investigate the features of the carbon dots as well as hybrid materials. The PL spectroscopy analysis indicated the in-vitro luminescent emission of the CQDs/45S5 nanohybrids compared to 45S5 bioglass. Around 22 μg of DOX could adhere to 1 mg of the nanohybrids after 12 h of contact, proposed that more quantity of particles were needed to adsorb higher amount of drug. The drug release study indicated a fast release pattern of DOX (90 %) during the first hours. The in-vitro bioactivity and biocompatibility results revealed the high capability of the BG/15 mL CQDs sample to deposit calcium phosphate with no toxic effects on the fibroblast cells. Consequently, the obtained nanohybrids could be promising trackable drug-vehicle candidates, as well as bone fillers, bone cements and implant coating materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Bioactive glass particles (BG) exhibit great potential for biomedical purposes, specifically in the realm of hard tissue regeneration. This is attributed to their exceptional capacity to bond with bones, compatibility with living tissues and their properties that facilitate the conduction and induction of bone growth [1]. The above-mentioned attributes render BG materials highly suitable for implementation as fundamental components in nanocomposites or as injectable biomaterials across diverse utilizations. Moreover, due to their diminutive particle size, BG nanoparticles can serve as effective vehicles for releasing biomolecules such as growth factors and anticancer drugs, catering to specific targeted objectives [2,3]. Hench reported 45S5, SiO2–CaO–Na2O–P2O5, as the initial bioactive material in 1969 [4]. Approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), this bioglass has undergone extensive research owing to its remarkable Class A bioactivity (able to bond both to soft and hard tissues) and a track record of successful clinical applications [5,6].

Class A bioactive glasses possess osteoconductive and osteoproductive properties, demonstrating the utmost capability for deposition of hydroxyapatite (HA), both in vitro and in vivo. This mineralization process is of vital importance for tissue engineering applications [7]. Nonetheless, extensive ongoing research is being conducted regarding the addition of bioglass to other compounds, including bioactive composite scaffolds [8] and glass ionomer cements [9]. The 45S5 bioactive glass is a primary composition under current investigation for the development of advanced biomaterials aimed at preventing tissue loss. This highlights the significance and immense potential of this bioglass for future applications [10].

Quantum dots (QDs) are nanocrystals in the size of 2–10 nm, exhibiting exceptional photoelectric properties that make them highly promising for energy-related applications [11]. Furthermore, QDs find applications in bioimaging, enabling real-time observation of intracellular functions and in vivo molecular imaging [12]. Significantly, the luminescent properties of quantum dots (QDs) can be preserved even when they are combined with other materials such as bioactive glass nanoparticles [13]. Moreover, due to the high rate of degradation in acidic medium and low degradation rate in neutral or alkaline media, quantum dots (QDs) can serve as cover to seal nanopores in drug carriers [14]. This enables controlled release of loaded drugs in low pH environments, which are typically accompanied by inflammation, bacterial infection, or cancer [15].

The widespread utilization of composite particles as targeted drug vehicles was impeded by the difficulty of tracking their movement and the delivered drug molecules within the body. To address these issues, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were integrated with the drug carriers. This integration aimed to overcome the tracking limitations and enhance the efficiency of drug delivery. On the other hand, compared to general CDs, bioactive CDs exhibited better bioactivity and biocompatibility. They had good osteogenic and cellular activities, which could promote the regeneration of new tissues [16]. In a study by Pardo et al. [17], the successful fabrication of biocompatible and remarkably fluorescent hybrid nanostructures, combining carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with hydroxyapatite (HA), was reported. These nanostructures were employed in the treatment of cancer cells. The incorporation of CQDs allowed for drug tracking by HA nanomaterials, leveraging the fluorescent properties of CQDs. Additionally, the presence of a HA nanoparticle shell provided protection and enhanced the fluorescence emission of CQDs [18].

The remarkable biocompatibility and luminescent characteristics of carbon dot composites were positioned these compounds as promising candidates in programmable, detectable, and targeted delivery systems. Notably, Sarkar et al. [19] and Yang et al. [20] were successfully prepared needle-like and ball-shape CD-HAP composites, respectively. These nanostructures exhibit remarkable drug loading potential and regulated delivery pattern, further enhancing their potential for drug delivery applications. The CQDs-HA-Alginate nanocomposites were developed in our group with remarkable biocompatibility and photoluminescence properties for bone tissue engineering approaches [21]. Khajuria et al. developed multifunctional NCD-HAp nanoparticles, where the combination of NCDs and HAp showed significant increase in cell proliferation, ALP activity and mineralization [22].

Carbon dots bioimaging has received tremendous attention over last decade since it allows non-invasive monitoring with highly biocompatible particles. Hence, creating novel bi-functional smart materials that offer bioimaging in advance of further bio-functions as bone regeneration or drug release, may open new horizons in treatment visualization. Overall, the novelty of the present work does not lay on the materials used since all three materials (bioglass and carbon dots) have been extensively studied, but on their combination in a single compound that not only assists bone regeneration but also permits simultaneous monitoring of the healing process and thus acting as a bi-functional smart material.

Taking into consideration the noteworthy biocompatibility and photoluminescence properties, CQDs-bioglass nanohybrids were developed for hard tissue engineering applications, here. There have been limited efforts conducted on the integration of carbon dots in the bioglass nanocomposites. Therefore, the present report aimed to prepare CQDs by employing citric acid through a hydrothermal method, followed by incorporation into the bioglass network using a co-precipitation process. The study thoroughly examined the effects of varying weight percentages of CQDs on the physical, chemical, and fluorescent features of the resulting nanohybrids.

The bioactive glasses were synthesized via the sol-gel method, following a conventional procedure used for synthesizing 45S5 bioglass [23]. The chemical precursors used in this process included tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as a source of silicon, triethyl phosphate (TEP) as a source of phosphate, calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2.4H2O), and sodium nitrate (NaNO3), which were obtained from Merck.

To catalyze the hydrolysis reaction, a citric acid (C6H8O7) solution in water was employed. A thermostat reactor was used to carefully regulate the temperature of the hydrolysis and condensation reactions.

figure S1 illustrated the flowchart depicting the fabrication process of 45S5 through the sol-gel method. The molar ratios of TEOS, TEP, NaNO3, and Ca(NO3)2·4H2O were carefully determined based on the molar ratios of SiO2, P2O5, Na2O, and CaO in 45S5. In order to obtain a transparent sol, the molar ratio of the aqueous acid solution to the four chemical precursors was adjusted to 10.

To begin with, the desired amount of acidic solution (26 mL) was placed in a thermostat reactor and stirred using a magnetic stirrer at the specified temperature. Gradually, TEOS (11.6 mL) and TEP (1 mL) were added drop by drop into the solution and continuously stirred to prepare a clear solution. Subsequently, the NaNO3 (4.66 g) was introduced into the stirring solution slowly to completely dissolve it. Subsequently, the Ca (NO3)2·4H2O (7.15 g) was mixed gradually with the solution and stirred for 1 h, resulting in the formation of a transparent sol. At this stage, the sol underwent a transformation into a gel via polycondensation reactions, which was maintained at 110 °C for duration of 12 h, followed by heat treatment at 600, 1000 and 1200 °C for 2 h in a furnace with 10 °C/min heating rate.

Synthesis of CQDs was performed via pyrolysis method involving citric acid, urea and polyethyleneimine (PEI). Specifically, an aqueous solution of urea, PEI and 0.6 g of citric acid was heated at 180 °C for 2 h in an oven and subsequently allowed to cool down in the air. To eliminate any unreacted components, the solution underwent dialysis against distilled water for 3 days. The external distilled water was replaced every 6 h during the dialysis process.

The chemical precipitation method was used to prepare CQDs/45S5 nanohybrids. To do so, various quantities (5, 15, and 30 mL) of the formerly made CQDs solution were mixed with an aqueous solution of citric acid under stirring to prepare a homogenous solution. Subsequently, the required materials for the synthesis of 45S5 bioglass were added, following the above-mentioned procedure. Via this method, the CQD nanoparticles could interact with the present ions in the solution and entrapped within the bioglass structure and form a nanohybrid.

The phase composition and crystallinity of the synthesized compounds were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (X′ Pert Pro, Philips BW3710). Cu Kα radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å was employed at 40 kV/30 mA, with a step size of 0.04° and a time per step of 0.5 s. The recorded spectra covered a 2θ range between 10° and 80°.

To analyze the chemical structure of the specimens, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy using a Rayleigh instrument from China was utilized. The spectroscopic measurements were carried out in the wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 1 cm−1. For the FT-IR measurement, a specific quantity of the specimen was blended with 200 mg of IR-grade KBr. This compound was then pressed into a translucent pellet using a stainless steel die. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the samples were examined using a Shimadzu RF-6000 Series spectrofluorophotometer, employing various excitation wavelengths. On the other hand, UV–vis analysis was conducted by employing a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrometer. The morphology of the prepared materials was assessed using field emission electron microscopy (FESEM) with a Zeiss HV-300 instrument from Germany. The hydrodynamic diameter (Size) and zeta potential (surface charge) of BG and CQDs/BG samples were determined using a dynamic light scattering instrument (DLS, Malvern Panalytical, Worcestershire, UK). Additionally, an energy dispersive X-ray analyzer from Oxford (AZtec1-England) was utilized to investigate the energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) and elemental mapping. Before SEM analysis, all samples underwent gold sputtering for 30–60 s at a current of 20 mA, resulting in an approximate gold thickness of 5–10 nm.

The bioactivity of the prepared samples was evaluated upon immersion in simulated body fluid (SBF) for 21 days. The SBF was prepared based on the usual kokubo method [24]. Formation of an apatite layer on the samples surface after 21 days indicated the level of bioactivity, which was analyzed by SEM and EDX.

Cytotoxicity of the obtained specimens was evaluated via MTT (3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay by employing human fibroblasts (A-431 cell line) provided by the cell bank of Iran Pasteur Institute. The cells viability depicts the composites biocompatibility [25].

In brief, 104 fibroblast/well were seeded into a 96-well plate containing DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal calf serum, incubated at 37 °C with 100 % relative humidity and 5 % CO2 for several hours, followed by treating with various concentrations (25, 50 and 100 μg/ml) of the samples. The empty wells were considered as the control groups in the assay. After 1 day of incubation, the supernatant was taken out of the wells. Prior to subjecting the specimens to MTT test, an inverted microscope (CKX, Olympus) was utilized to observe the density of cells in each well and capture images at 40 × . Then, 0.5 % MTT solution was added to the plates and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. After aspirating the culture medium, 0.2 ml of DMSO was poured into each well and the UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used to read the absorbance of the final solution at 492 nm. Each test was carried out in triplicate and the viable cells were quantified based on the absorbance values. The measured values for control were considered as 100 % to determine the cell viability percentage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doxorubicin Hydrochloride loading and release study
ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin Hydrochloride (DOX, Actoverco pharmaceutical company. Iran), an anticancer antibiotic drug known for its effectiveness and affordability, was chosen as the representative antibiotic for this study. A stock solution was prepared by dissolving 10 mg of the drug in 10 ml of PBS, which was then diluted to achieve a solution of 100 μg/mL concentration. Samples weighing 1 mg were suspended in the drug solution within 4 mL microtubes and subjected to agitation for duration of 2 days. At specific times, the supernatants were collected for quantitative analysis. The quantification of DOX adsorbed on the samples was indirectly performed by determining the content of the drug in the medium, prior to and subsequent to the loading test.

To evaluate the in-vitro drug release behavior, previously prepared drug-loaded samples were placed in various tubes filled with 5 mL PBS solution at 37 °C and a pH of 7.4. The tubes were gently shaken. To measure the amount of the released drug, various specimens were obtained at various time points and analyzed using UV–Vis spectrophotometry at a wavelength of 480 nm. The drug release evaluation was conducted in triplicate, and the percentage of released DOX was plotted over time.
The concentration of DOX at time interval was determined by using a standard calibration curve, which was created by measuring the absorbance of various concentrations of drug in PBS.

The X-ray diffraction analysis was employed to study the crystal structure of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1 B, the broad peak around 25 ⁰ relating to (002) plane of graphite, resembled the partial graphitization and tiny scale CQDs [26]. The non-clear peak around 45⁰ could be ascribed to the (101) plane resulted from the in-plane diffraction of graphene-like structured CQDs. Additionally, the reduced sharpness and enhanced full-width half maxima of this pattern denoted the amorphous structure of the obtained CQDs [27]. Different functional groups on CQDs were studied by FTIR analysis, as presented in Fig. 1A and Table 1 . The observed sharp peaks around 1697 and 1387 cm−1 could be corresponded to the –C=O and C=C vibrations, respectively [28]. The bands around 3100-3500 cm−1 signified the presence of O–H or N–H stretching [21,29]. The narrow band at 1139 cm−1 could be due to the C–O–C bonds [29]. Based on the AFM and TEM results in Fig. 1C and D, the obtained CQDs were in nano-scale where most of them were around 11 nm in scale (Fig. 1E). The lattice fringes in Fig. 1D with a spacing of 0.223 nm are consistent with 100 facets of graphite [30].

The sample for AFM was prepared by leaving a drop on a freshly cleaved mica and the image was provided after evaporation of the solvent. Since the substrate for the sample is almost flat, the height profile seen in the image (Fig. 1C) is an indication of the height of the dots or roughly said, the diameter of the dots.

The XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 2 offers compelling signs of the glass-ceramic structure of the sample, indicating that the 45S5 glass underwent devitrification through the sintering process. This observation aligns with previous findings from various studies, which consistently demonstrate the potential of 45S5 to crystallize at high temperatures of 550 °C [31,32]. By increasing the sintering temperature to 1000 °C, the intensity of the main peak around 2θ ~ 34° showed a noticeable rise, and gradually, a secondary peak became evident. The primary crystalline phase identified in Fig. 2 corresponds to combeite (Na2CaSi2O6, PDF code 01-077-2189), while silico-rhenanite (Na2Ca4(PO4)2SiO4, PDF code 00-032-1053) was also observed as a secondary phase. The obtained data were in excellent agreement with prior research investigating the sinter-crystallization behavior of 45S5 Bioglass® powder [33,34]. 45S5 was melted after heat treatment of at 1200 °C (XRD pattern not shown).

As also reported by Lefebvre et al. [31] and Lin et al. [35], Na2CaSi2O6, which is the predominant crystalline phase observed at 610 °C, is discerned as Na2CaSi2O6 instead of Na2Ca2Si3O9. Na2CaSi2O6 shares the same crystal structure as the high temperature variant of Na2Ca2Si3O9 (where 2 Na ions replace 1 Ca ion), which provides a possible explanation for the initial belief that 45S5 crystallized in the Na2Ca2Si3O9 phase [36]. There are two supporting points indicating Na2CaSi2O6 as the preferred crystalline phase over Na2Ca2Si3O9. Firstly, the composition of Bioglass® is more similar to Na2CaSi2O6. Secondly, the Rietveld evaluation demonstrates a stronger correspondence between the experimental diffraction pattern and the pattern calculated for Na2CaSi2O6. Nevertheless, a comprehensive characterization of the crystal structure, encompassing atom arrangements and accounting for potential solid solutions, is still incomplete.

In order to identify the present phases in the BG/CQDs nanohybrid, the powder X-ray diffraction technique was employed. Fig. 3 demonstrated the x-ray patterns of the prepared BG/CQDs nanohybrids under different heat treatment conditions. Interestingly, it could be observed that the appeared peaks in the pattern of the dried sample at around 100 °C could reflect the formation of calcium carbonate polymorphs (JCPDS files: aragonite: 5–0453; calcite: 83–0578; vaterite: 25–0127) [37]. The reaction between the dissolved CO2 gas and calcium ions might play an important role in this phenomenon [38]. The strong and sharp peaks also demonstrated the well-crystallized phases. Lakshmi et al. [39] also reported the phase evolution of calcium carbonate to bioglass after calcination at different temperatures.

Fig. 4 and Table 2 display FTIR data that showcase the distinct peaks associated with various vibrations. These include Si–O–Si bending, symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations, observed at 517, 618, and 900–1000 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 618 cm−1 corresponds to the P–O stretching vibration. Additionally, the narrow bands observed around 1350–1450 cm−1 indicated the presence of the carbonate group (CO3 2−) [40,41].

The new absorption characteristic bands appeared in the FTIR spectrum of BG/CQDs sample around 1570-1640 cm−1 could be ascribed to the –C=O groups, as also reported by Ma et al. [18]. The characteristic strong adsorption bands around 1837 cm−1 could be assigned to C─O─C stretching vibration in CQD and a peak in 1361 cm−1 due to the presence of carboxylic functional groups [42]. The observed variations in the bands positions and intensities between two spectra could be related to the chemical interactions of –COOH and –CH groups on CQDs with bioglass active sites.

Quantum dot (QD) is a semiconductor crystal having dimensions in the nanometer scale. QDs can be synthesized from metals (e.g. Au), carbon based materials (e.g. carbon dots and graphene) or semiconductors (e.g. Se, Cd etc.). Quantum dots have high self-luminescence and having specific size range, they possess quantum phenomena that yield benefits in optical properties. Upon excitation, smaller QDs emit light with higher energy and intensity [43,44]. These are zero-dimensional (0 D) luminescent nanomaterials that display quantum confinement and size effect. Depending on the size, functional groups and excitation wavelength, the fluorescent emission wavelengths can exhibit different coloured lights, like deep ultraviolet, blue, green, yellow or red [45,46].

The high luminescence, narrow emission, and (depending on the elemental composition) low toxicity and biocompatibility of QDs make them a perfect candidate for bioimaging, diagnostics, and biosensing applications, mostly related to biomedical and environmental sciences [47].

Fig. 5 B displayed the normalized photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs under excitation at various wavelengths ranging from 280 to 400 nm. The emission peak wavelengths remained largely consistent regardless of the irradiation excitation wavelength, which might be attributed to the confined range of particles size and generally comparable surface chemistry of the prepared CQDs. The emission peak exhibiting the maximum intensity was centered at 450 nm wavelength for excitation at 380 nm threshold.

As depicted in Fig. 5A inset, the aqueous solution of CQDs presented a blue emission under UV irradiation (365 nm), indicating the fluorescent characteristic of the obtained material. The UV–Vis spectroscopy data for the prepared sample, shown in Fig. 5A also exhibited absorption peaks at 218 nm and 365 nm, where the peak at 218 could be attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C bonds [48], while the peak around 365 nm likely corresponded to the oxygen-related surface states involving the n-π* transition of –C=O bond [49].

The absorbance spectra of the synthesized materials were recorded by using UV–Vis spectroscopy to investigate the electronic transitions taking place (Fig. 6 ). The plots clearly demonstrate changes in peak height and variation in transition energies, indicating the potential occurrence of an interaction between the bioglass and C-dots [50].

Fig. 7 exhibited the PL spectra of BG as well as those of BG/5 ml CQDs, BG/15 ml CQDs, and BG/30 ml CQDs nanohybrids, using excitation at various wavelengths between 200 and 400 nm. Optical photographs of the samples obtained under UV irradiation were shown as insets in the plots. In comparison with the PL spectrum of pure BG, which showed almost no PL emission (Fig. 7A), the materials that were functionalized with CQDs exhibited intense photoluminescence (PL) emission across both the ultraviolet and visible ranges of the electromagnetic spectrum. The PL strength was found to be highest when all samples were excited by irradiation at 380 nm. However, the position of the peak was changed at other excitation wavelengths (were not centered at 450 nm), suggested the alteration of the energy levels upon formation of the hybrid system. The obtained data were consistent with the FTIR results, as it was stated that variations in the functional groups can change the positions of PL emission peaks of CQDs [51].

The observed signal distortion in the spectra of nanohybrid (BG/5 ml CQDs) at some irradiation wavelengths could be related to muddy state of the samples upon dispersion in distilled water. However they almost preserved the shape and intensity, indicating that the sample still possess good PL property. As shown in the inset plots, photoluminescence emission from the CQD-loaded nanohybrids could clearly be seen under ultraviolet light excitation, which was the highest for BG/30 mL CQDs sample. The optical photographs of all samples under UV irradiation can be found in the supplementary section (Fig. S2) for comparison.

The khajuria et al. [22] also expressed that the nitrogen-doped carbon dots functionalized with hydroxyapatite nanoparticles had fluorescence emission in the range of 440–610 nm, which was lower than pristine CDs. They reported that the reduction of the fluorescence intensity is an indirect evidence for the deposition of CDs on HA nanoparticles. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-HA nanoparticles increased with the increase in the % deposition of CDs on HA nanoparticles, which was in agreement with our findings.

Fluorescence intensity mainly depends on existence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs that provide the energy level. Chung et al. [52] demonstrated that the increase in PL intensity of CDs/HA after addition of higher amount of CD s might be due to the existence of more functional groups that are responsible for the fluorescence behaviour of the nanocomposite.

Based on the existing literature, it was stated that the texture of bioglass can affect its bioactivity, where the presence of porosity and crystallization in the material can accelerate the apatite materialization speed [53,54]. Therefore, the morphology of the resulting samples was thoroughly examined using FESEM and visualized in Fig. 8 . Formation of bioglass nanoparticles was clearly observed in Fig. 8A, however agglomeration of small particles after heating the powders at 600 °C was led to the formation of bigger particles. The powders sintered at 1000 °C were almost melted and individual particles were not detectable. Upon heating the 45S5 powder in furnace at 1200 °C, the bioglass was completely melted. Addition of 5 and 15 mL of carbon dots to the bioglass powders was resulted in the formation of agglomerated structure, which was transformed to micro-scale particles upon calcination at 600 °C (Fig. 9 B and C) and grain growth. Heat treatment of these nanohybrids at 1000 °C could evolve porous structures, which might happen due to the removal of CQDs nanoparticles [55].

The micrographs of the sample contained 30 mL CQDs exhibited the agglomeration of nano-scale flaky crystals at 600 °C (Fig. 8D), suggesting that the high quantity of carbon dots could suppress the bioglass grains growth during sintering and create a pore-free hybrid with sub-200-nm fine crystals. On the other site, since the bioglass precursors have come in contact with CQDs nanoparticles during the synthesis of BG/CQDs nanohybrids and actually the CQDs have played as nucleation sites for the growth of glass crystals. So, in BG/30 mL CQDs sample where much more nucleation sites have been available compared with the BG/5 mL CQDs and BG/15 mL CQDs, smaller particle size was achieved, as clearly shown in Fig. 8B, C and D after heating at 600 °C.

Chung et al. [52] have synthesized rod-like CD-HAP nanocomposite with 142 ± 20 nm length and width of 20 ± 10 nm. Similar works have also been reported by Sarkar et al. [19] and Ma et al. [18], showed average size of 60 ± 15 nm and100 nm with a rod-like shape CD-HAP nanocomposite too.

It has been stated that the bulk materials composed of nano powders exhibit fine and homogeneous microstructure. Moreover, agglomeration and coalescence of particles in the hybrid material that occurred due to the high content of CQDs, could facilitate the sintering process at higher temperatures (1000 °C) and form denser product [56]. Furthermore, presence of smaller particles and more grain boundaries can limit the atomic movements during heating of the material, which preserve the structure and postpone the melting process. In this regard, increase in the content of CQDs in the hybrids was resulted in decrease in the particle size, as could be clearly observed in Fig. 8 after heating at 1000 °C, where the BG/30 mL CQDs samples had the smallest particle size. According to the FESEM images in Fig. 8, it could be suggested that the average size of synthesized BG and BG/30 mL CQDs samples were in the nanometer range.

The accompanied EDS data (Fig. S3and table S1) disclosed the elemental analysis of the prepared BG/CQDs nanohybrids. The quantity of the carbon atoms was increased as the quantity of CQDs increased from BG/5 mL CQDs sample to BG/30 mL CQDs. According to the mapping examination, the calcium, sodium, oxygen, silicon, and phosphorus were found in the prepared bioglass (Fig. 8A). but the map analysis could not detect the carbon, whereas, well distribution of carbon atoms were observed in the structure of the bioglass/CQDs samples, as shown in Fig. 8 insets.

After immersion of the bioglass in SBF, silica structure collapses due to the loss of ions, which results in the formation of silanol with negative charge on the surface. Subsequently, re-polymerization of silanols forms a silica-rich layer on the glass surface. This silicon rich layer adsorbs the calcium and phosphate ions and forms a CaO–P2O5 film. These assembles ions create a calcium phosphate layer on the submerged surface, which then transforms into a calcium deficient carbonate apatite layer, similar to the inorganic part of the natural bone. It has been widely studied that formation of a bone-like apatite layer on the bioactive materials surface is the prerequisite of the binding to living bone in the biological environment [57,58]. So, the prepared specimen was immersed in SBF for 21 days to study their bioactivity via formation of an apatite layer.

The surface morphology of the BG/15 mL CQDs powders was completely changed after the SBF in-vitro test. The nucleated minerals on the surface were clearly observed in spherical white color (Fig. 9 A). Based on the EDS examination, the calcium and phosphorus contents were higher in the spectrum 3 (Fig. 9 d) (Atomic % of Ca: 20.01 and P: 0.31), compared with the other two spectrums (Fig. 9b and c), which indicated the precipitation of calcium phosphate crystals on the surface of the sample.

The materials used in tissue engineering should represent the least cytotoxicity. It is well known that CQDs and bioglass have excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, plus their easy production and low-cost [59]. However, their hybridization may affect the toxicity of the final product. In this regard, the potential cytotoxicity of the obtained BG/15 mL CQDs sample was evaluated via MTT method, where different concentrations of the extracted liquid of the specimens came in contact with the fibroblast cells as shown in figures S4.

The observed enhancement in the proliferation of fibroblasts in contact with higher concentration (50 and 100 μg/mL) of the extracted liquid was supposed to be related to the favorable cell signaling of these compounds. It was reported that the ionic products came from the dissolved material, especially Si and Ca from bioactive glasses are able to motivate the proliferation of osteoblasts and expression of genes [60–62].

The results suggest that the prepared hybrids in this work could be safely employed in vivo at different concentrations, although further evaluation of the hybrids cytocompatibility is required, involving in vivo experiments, in order to ascertain that they are safe for application in humans.

Regarding the obtained photoluminescence data, the BG/15 mL CQDs and BG/30 mL CQDs samples demonstrated the best results and therefore, these samples were selected for the drug adsorption and release study. Fig. 10 A indicated the adsorption isotherms of DOX on the BG/15 mL CDQs and BG/30 mL CQDs nanohybrids. The highest adsorption capacity was observed after 12 h of contact between the samples and the drug solutions, which was 22 % of the drug on the BG/15 mL CDQs specimen and very low amount of drug for BG/30 mL CQDs specimen. After 48 h of contact, the amount of the adsorbed drug did not change dramatically, which indicated that all the active sites on the samples were occupied by the drug molecules during the first hours and no more free sites were available to increase the adsorption percentage. The observed high adsorption capacity of BG/15 mL CDQs sample could be attributed to the porous structure of the hybrid material, which could accommodate drug molecules. The drug loading efficiency was measured around 0.022 mg drug/1 mg particles after 12 h of contact. Whereas, almost no drug molecules were attached to the BG/30 mL CQDs specimen, proposed the low affinity of this hybrid to the DOX drug. It has been previously observed that a network of intra- and inter-granular pores forms during the formation of wollastonite particles from calcium silicate hydrate, due to the removal of water vapor after heating at high temperatures vapors [63,64].

As represented in Fig. 10 B, almost 90 % of the DOX was desorbed after 6 h and the remained drug was not released during the next hours, indicated that some of the drug molecules were stocked within pores and could not come out easily.

Short and predictable delivery pattern is favorable for medicinal functions of specific drugs such as antibiotics [65], but strategies to increase the release time up to days or weeks can improve the potential of nanoparticles as a delivery system. A prolonged release may also let better control of the drug release mechanism. On the other hand, the burst release of drugs is not desirable in most diseases, which require a long time healing process, but for the anti-infection applications, the burst release of drug can limit the infection, in some hours time [66]. Ahmed El-Fiqi et al. [3] also employed mesoporous bioglass as ampicillin drug carrier. Release of about 80 % of the loaded drug after 12 h in phosphate buffer indicated that the drug molecules could not attaché strongly to the bioglass particles and were vulnerable to ion exchange.

In order to extend the release time, the obtained nanohybrid (BG/CQD/DOX) is suggested to be blended with a hydrolytically stable polymer with low degradation rate. In this way, slow degradation of polymer can inhibit the fast release of drug. A similar research has been previously conducted by our group and the polymeric chains could retain the drug molecules for several days [67].

Integration of CQDs into the 45S5 bioglass was performed successfully via an easy and environmental friendly procedure. Different quantities of CQDs were used in the BG/CQDs hybrids and then heat treated at different temperatures to evaluate the structural, morphological and photoluminescent variations, as well as drug loading and release behavior. Overall, 12 samples were prepared and characterized in details. According to the XRD patterns, 45S5 bioglass was successfully synthesized, where the phase transformations was in agreement with previous reports. Based on the obtained data, the BG/15 mL CQDs and the BG/30 mL CQDs samples represented high photoluminescence emissions, whereas the BG/15 mL CQDs sample exhibited higher DOX adsorption capacity compared with the other samples. Attachment of around 20 % of the drug on this sample was ascribed to the porous structure that could mechanically and electrostatically hold the drug molecules. Almost 90 % of the loaded DOX was released in 6 h, resembled a fast release pattern. In spite of pharmacological drawbacks of the burst release, it can be an ideal policy in some situations, especially for instant relief of pain and targeting specific cells to inhibit proliferation. Therefore, among all samples, the BG/15 mL CQDs nanohybrid heat treated at 600 °C was supposed to have the potential to be used as bone drug carries. In order to scrutinize this assumption, the in-vitro bioactivity of the sample was carried out to evaluate the bone-bonding ability of the material.

The BG/15 mL CQDs sample exhibited good biomineralisation responses upon immersion in SBF and apatite crystals were formed on the sample. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of the obtained material was studied by MTT assay, which represented no toxic effects on the fibroblasts cell line after 24 h. To the best of our knowledge, this was the first study on BG/CQDs hybrids that investigated the effect of heat treatment on the structural and morphological variations and linked these properties to the drug carrying potential. In conclusion, this study confirmed the capability of the BG/CQDs nanohybrids in bone tissue engineering applications, especially bone filling material with drug loading capacity. However, investigating the effect of CQDs content on the hybrids microstructure, drug adsorption profiles, desorption mechanisms and more biological interactions are features that merit further study.

Moreover and as the future scope, BG/CQDs can be combined with Plasmonic metal nanoparticles for plasmon-enhanced fluorescence bioimaging or biosensor. In this way, the plasmonic properties and biocompatibility of metal nanoparticles is combined with the extremely bright, stable, and size-tunable fluorescence properties of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), which may quench or enhance photoluminescence (PL) of QDs via nanosurface energy transfer (NSET). The Photonic crystal are able to drastically alter the light-emitting characteristics of adjacently located luminescent species with photonic-plasmonics coupling [68,69].
